As it went farther away from the earth terrestrial attraction would be diminished in inverse proportion to the square of distances, but the lunar attraction would be augmented in the same proportion. A point must, therefore, be reached where these two attractions would neutralise each other, and the bullet would have no weight at all. If the volumes of the moon and earth were equal, this point would have been reached at an equal distance between the two bodies. They are said to follow ships in search of refreshing shade from the hot tropical sun, and they did just that with the Nautilus, as they had once done with the vessels of the Count de La Pérouse. For long hours they competed in speed with our submersible. I couldn't stop marveling at these animals so perfectly cut out for racing, their heads small, their bodies sleek, spindle-shaped, and in some cases over three meters long, their pectoral fins gifted with remarkable strength, their caudal fins forked. Like certain flocks of birds, whose speed they equal, these tuna swim in triangle formation, which prompted the ancients to say they'd boned up on geometry and military strategy. And yet they can't escape the Provençal fishermen, who prize them as highly as did the ancient inhabitants of Turkey and Italy; and these valuable animals, as oblivious as if they were deaf and blind, leap right into the Marseilles tuna nets and perish by the thousands.

Once the lunar disc could no longer be reached (RM-010-002), the travellers' survival, not their landing, becomes the story's true concern.
[FWB-043-032]In the lapse of fifteen minutes [[victoria-balloon|the Victoria]] was only one hundred and fifty feet above the ground; but the wind was much stronger than before.

The lunatic throws out the last ballast so that "we must have now reached a height of at least nine thousand yards," and blood spurts from the narrator's nose and mouth (DA-001-160). Glorying that "the higher we go, the more glorious will be our death," he moves to cut the car loose to "approach the sun" (DA-001-197, DA-001-203).
- The engineer of the Mongolia — offered "a large reward... if he gets us to Bombay well in advance of time" (AWED-008-036)
It becomes the castaway's lookout and beacon, the point from which he scans the sea for any sail that might carry him home.
Its wooded back is later set ablaze — "a huge glare lighted up... the whole of the coast from Flag Point to the southern cape of Dream Bay." (GM-021-098)

Conseil and Ned Land debate the submarine's route by way of the cape. Conseil predicts "the Nautilus will go back down to the Indian Ocean, perhaps through Mozambique Channel... and then make for the Cape of Good Hope." (TTLU-028-012) Ned Land is aghast at the pace required "if it's to double the Cape of Good Hope, circle around Africa, and lie in the open Mediterranean by the day after tomorrow." (TTLU-028-088), demanding "what's this talk about doubling the Cape of Good Hope?" (TTLU-028-089)
id: ceylon

Charleston is the blockaded South Carolina port at the centre of *[[The Blockade Runners]]*, the city where the Civil War's first shot was fired at [[Fort Sumter]], a cotton-choked harbour whose blockade the Dolphin runs, ranked with [[New Orleans]] among the Confederate ports.
label: Villa Rica

The drifting mass keeps the name it bore as solid ground: its longitude could not be fixed at once, the original name of [[Victoria Island]] being retained (FC-025-003). As the season turns, there could be no doubt that the bitter cold of winter would solder Victoria Island to the vast ice-field (FC-025-015), fixing for a time the course of the wandering colony.

The mutiny is the pivot on which the whole polar quest turns, converting a well-found expedition into a fight for bare survival on the ice. Long afterward the loss still haunts its captain, who dreamt of his ship, the [[the-forward|Forward]], and of the traitors that had burnt it (ACH-054-029).
aliases: [The Balloon Drama, The Drama in the Air]

On the drifting ice-island, the sole man who held Erik's secret, Patrick O'Donoghan, died without revealing it (WC-020-002), and the villain Tudor Brown expired without a word (WC-020-003). Rescued at the cannon-shot of the *Alaska* (WC-020-007), the crew turned homeward.

The enclosed letter from Mr. Durrien began "My dear child," telling how he had read the biography translated from the Swedish and been overcome, recognising in it Erik's own account of himself (WC-021-008).
description: "The witty French war correspondent who reports the Tartar invasion, telegraphing his dispatches to an imaginary cousin."

He is in truth a foundling: it was known that "Erik believed himself to be your son, and that every one in Noroe had forgotten how he had become yours" (WC-002-071). Struck by the boy's promise — "Erik is twelve years old, nearly thirteen, and he appears to be highly gifted" (WC-003-036) — the doctor carries him off to Stockholm to be educated and to seek his origins.
label: Phina Hollaney
- [[The Adventures of Captain Hatteras]] — the Arctic expedition of the Forward, on whose polar dash "Bell started the first; the others followed" (ACH-028-014).

He is the chief officer of the doomed vessel, introduced by the narrator as "this mate, whose name is Robert Curtis, our crew consists of Walter, the lieutenant, the boatswain, and fourteen sailors" (SC2-002-003).
